text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. This is the second video in a larger series on reinforcement learning with LLMs. Here I'll discuss three ways RL is being used to train modern language models and to create domainspecific AI agents. So in the last video I talked about reinforcement learning and how it gives computers the ability to learn through trial and error. The basic way this works is that in RL we have an agent that's interacting with an environment and every time the agent does a good action it gets rewarded for it and every time the agent does a bad action it does not get rewarded. And to make this process a bit more formal last time we talked about a few key concepts in reinforcement learning. Namely, we call the agents interactions with the environment actions. The agent receives immediate rewards for those actions. Additionally, the actions will move the agent into a new so-called state and then given this new state, the agent will execute a new action based on its so-called policy. So, this is just something that takes in the agent's current state and outputs an action. While reinforcement learning is a very flexible and general framework for teaching computers how to do things, when we apply it to large language models, this picture actually gets a lot simpler. Instead of rewarding good actions, we reward good responses and we do not reward bad ones. And accordingly, the picture changes to the following. So instead of a state, we'll have a input request coming from the user that gets passed to the agent. Instead of a policy, we just have a large language model that takes in the request and generates a response. Instead of generating actions, the LLM will just respond to the request. And every response will receive a reward based on its quality. But we can actually simplify this a little bit here. We can basically think of the agent and the LLM as the same thing. So this simplifies it a little bit. Additionally, even though we can have LLMs interacting with the real world through tool calls, most of the time we can just simplify the picture even more and we can just think of the LLM as receiving requests, generating responses to those requests and receiving a reward for that response. So a key point that differentiates reinforcement learning from supervised fine-tuning or self-supervised training of large language models is that the feedback and the training signal that the LLM is receiving is on the response level rather than the token level. So in self-supervised learning or in supervised fine-tuning, the LLM is given an input sequence and it is trained to predict the most likely next token based on the training data. And this is why LLMs generate text one token at a time. However, with reinforcement learning, we don't evaluate each individual token. Rather, we evaluate the entire response that the LLM generates. This gives us a more natural way to give feedback to LLM responses because it's much easier to read a response and say whether that response was good or bad as opposed to evaluating every single individual token in a given response. While reinforcement learning has gained a lot of popularity over the past couple of years and has really been used to train LLMs since the beginning and by that I mean since the development of chatbt in 2022. So there, OpenAI used a three-part training strategy which started with so-called pre-training and this was a self-supervised learning step of basically getting a bunch of unlabeled text data from the internet and training a model to predict what comes next. So given an input sequence, predicting the next piece of text in that sequence and then the next two stages are under this umbrella of post-raining. So the first part of post- training was supervised fine-tuning. So taking this basically internet document completer and turning it into something that follows instructions and answers user questions and then doing one last step of reinforcement learning with human feedback. Even though the model can answer user questions in this chat format, that doesn't necessarily mean that the responses are helpful or aligned with human preferences and human values. That's why reinforcement learning with human feedback was such a critical step. However, since then, a lot has changed in the development of large language models. While we still have these two key phases of pre-training and post-training, there's a lot more involved in this process. There still will be self-supervised learning, but there's also now the emergence of so-called mid-training, which will be like a curation of higher quality unlabeled data. Other things that might be included at mid-training are instruction tuning. So basically teaching the model how to do some early versions of instruction following and expanding its context window. So enabling long context and then post- training can involve a lot of different things. Instruction tuning and preference tuning are what we saw with Chad GBT but now there are a lot of new things we're seeing. There's often a step to teach the model how to reason or to think. Most models these days are reasoning models. Basically they can think before responding. Also tool calling has become table stakes with large language models. So most models are trained to deal with structured outputs or to call tools. It's also pretty common to do distillation. So generating data from existing models and using them to fine-tune a new model. And then finally doing safety and alignment fine-tuning. There's definitely a lot that can go into creating a large language model. And while there are some patterns to this whole process, it still does seem like all the different labs do things slightly differently. But the key thing I want to point out here is all the different ways reinforcement learning appears in the training process. Here I'm going to talk about three main ways that reinforcement learning is used to train large language models. I'll start with reinforcement learning with human feedback which is what was used in chat GPT. I'll also talk about reinforcement learning with AI feedback which builds on top of RLHF and then finally I'll talk about reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards which is the most recent and perhaps the most exciting of the three. So starting with RLHF, the goal here is to align an LLM's responses with human preferences. This is the same picture we saw earlier. So we send a request to a LLM, it generates a response and the entire response receives a single reward. So in RLHF the reward is basically something like a probability that a user will like the response. However, since it's hard to get highquality data of human labels of responses, instead of using human labels directly in this loop, it uses a so-called reward model to give feedback to the LLM. The punch line here is that through this process, good responses will get rewarded so that similar responses to similar requests will be more likely in the future. So, a good question here is why do we even need reinforcement learning? Can we just come up with examples of good responses and have the LLM learn from those? And the answer is yes. We can definitely use supervised fine-tuning and indeed this is what people do for training LLMs. However, there's one advantage of doing RLHF with this LLM reward model feedback loop. Ultimately, it's easier to evaluate good responses than it is to write them from scratch. So you can imagine for RLHF instead of writing responses from scratch, we just need to get responses from an LLM and give it a thumbs up or a thumbs down. Since this is a much easier task, we can generate much more training data which ultimately results in a better, more aligned model. Just to give you a flavor of how this works, I'm going to walk through how this reward model is actually developed. The first step is the data labeling process here. The way that works is that a request will be passed into a large language model and then instead of just generating one response, the LLM will generate multiple responses. So this could be anywhere from like five to 20 responses or however much we like. Then all these responses are given to a human labeler who's tasked with ranking them. And then once we have these rankings, we can pass pairs of conversations to a reward model and have it assign a reward for each conversation. And then the training process consists of giving higher rewards to these higher ranked responses. Basically, the way this works is by training the model to maximize the difference between these two rewards such that the higher ranked reward is assigned a higher score. And then once we have this reward model, we can use it to improve the LLM in that reinforcement learning loop we saw earlier. Although it is easier for human labelers to generate these response rankings than it is for them to write example responses from scratch, relying on humans in this training loop still introduces a bottleneck. This brings up the second way reinforcement learning is used to train LLMs, which is through so-called reinforcement learning with AI feedback or RL Aif. And the basic idea here is to just replace the human labelers with an LLM. Here we have that same exact training loop. So we have a request, we give it to an LLM, it generates a response and that response is scored by a reward model. However, the key difference here is that instead of creating the reward model based on human annotated data or human rankings, the reward model is trained on data of LLM rankings. Just to show you how that works here again, we have that data labeling process we saw with RHF. So, we have a request that's passed into an LLM that generates multiple responses. But now instead of passing these responses to a human labeler, they're passed to an LLM labeler that has access to strict guidelines and examples of how to evaluate these responses. At this point, you might look at this picture and be kind of confused. You might say, "Well, it doesn't really sound like we're getting human values and human preferences here because the LLM has its own values and preferences. And even if we give it very detailed guidelines, how can it know like all the subtleties and nuances of what humans like? Even humans don't understand why they prefer one thing over another. This is why RLif is helpful for more narrow tasks like evaluating harmfulness or helpfulness where there is a concrete rubric for evaluating these responses. With this more constrained problem, we can get more reliable labels from the AI. Then from here we can train the reward model in exactly the same way as RHF. Although RLHF and RLIF can both be used for preference tuning and alignment fine-tuning, they do have some key differences. This is a comparison table from reference number six. The obvious difference between these two approaches is that RHF uses human annotators while RLIif uses an AI model. But some like practical differences is from a scalability perspective, RLIF is much easier to scale which is its key benefit while RLHF is slow and the key bottleneck is hiring humans and training them to do this task. Also, relying on humans costs money while LLMs are much cheaper and then having humans in the loop makes the annotation cycles much slower while with LLMs you can generate annotations very quickly. So, so far it might sound like RLif is a amazing approach, but of course there are some challenges with it. One of the challenges is coming up with good guidelines for the model to generate its labels. This is going to be limited by how well you can write that prompt for the LLM. While with human labelers, you can give them guidelines and even if there are some gaps and edge cases, they can use their personal experience to fill in the gaps of what makes sense in a specific context. Ultimately, this results in RLHF being described as high noise and low bias. So, in other words, the labels are inconsistent because humans have all different kinds of opinions, but low bias because if you're sampling the right human annotators, then their preferences should also reflect the preferences of the end user. However, using an LLM for this results in low noise and high bias. In other words, it's low noise because the LLM is very consistent, but the AI is going to have some systematic biases just because of its training. Both approaches have their strengths and their challenges. So, usually both are going to be used in post-training. The final way reinforcement learning is being used to train LLMs is so-called reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards. The key idea here is to train the model to achieve outcomes rather than just generate good responses. The training loop is going to look very similar. We'll have a request get passed to an LLM that will generate a response. However, now instead of evaluating the response using a reward model, the correctness of the response is evaluated using code. And since we're evaluating correctness, this is going to require that the request is asking the model to produce some kind of fact or mathematical truth that's not open to interpretation. Usually, this is going to be some kind of math, coding, or logic problem that does have a verifiable right answer. Just to give you a flavor of how this works, there was a landmark paper published by DeepSeek in early 2025 that was the first open-source demonstration that this idea actually worked. And so there the request to the LLM looked something like this. A conversation between user and assistant. The user asks a question and the assistant solves it. The assistant first thinks about the reasoning process in mind and then provides the user with the answer. The reasoning process and answer are enclosed within think and answer tags respectively. I.e. think reasoning process here, answer here and then we have a problem provided by a user and then the assistant response. The key things to highlight here is that there are these special tags that are introduced in this prompt that are telling the LLM to put its so-called reasoning process between think tags and to put the answer that we're going to evaluate in answer tags. The other key point is that this user question, this is going to be from like a set of math coding and or logic problems that are dynamically injected into this prompt. To give you an example, this might be something like today's date is written as 2211 2015. How many other days in this year can be written using the same set of digits? And then the behavior of the model that we expect is something like this. So, it's going to start with a think tag and it's going to start generating tokens. So, it's basically just thinking through the problem and then it's going to keep thinking until the LLM works it out. It will end its thinking and then it will provide an answer like this. The key point here is by having these think tags, we can have the model go off and explore different ideas and think through the problem as much as it likes before coming up with an answer. And then it turns out as the model is trained for longer and longer, these reasoning traces get longer and longer. Basically, the model learns that thinking longer tends to lead to better responses. And this is shown here. As the models train longer and longer, we can see that its performance is slowly improving. This phenomenon of longer reasoning traces leading to better performance is called test time compute scaling. So basically, the more tokens the model generates, the better its performance. The exciting thing about RLVR is that this opened the door for longunning autonomous agents and sparked a new era of AI agents back in 2025. There we saw the rise of taskspecific and domainspecific agents that were fine-tuned through endtoend reinforcement learning. The first big success was deep research which is now basically everywhere. But initially it was this feature in chatbt where you could give it a prompt. It would ask you some follow-up questions and then would go off and do some research for like 10 or 15 minutes and get back to you. More recently, we've seen the influx of these coding agents. So things like Codeex, Claude Code, Cursor's coding agent, and many others. These agents are really good at developing software and they can often run for several minutes at a time and sometimes even for hours or days. But this idea can go beyond deep research and coding agents to creating custom AI agents in basically any domain or for basically any task. For example, Openpipe made this art agent which was basically an email agent. So they did end-to-end reinforcement fine-tuning to teach it how to be a helpful email assistant. This idea can be applied to creating support agents with complex tool calling or you can teach the model how to edit photos or even edit videos by giving access to tools and training it in this endtoend way. For any task where there is a concrete right answer and you can use that to give a LLM feedback. It's possible to create a custom agent using reinforcement learning in this end-to-end way. However, there are indeed some limitations with doing reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards. So, the first is that reinforcement learning is quite computationally expensive and unstable. So, it can take a lot of trial and error to get something right. And while you're figuring it all out, you're going to burn through a lot of resources. There's also this problem of sparse rewards, also called the credit assignment problem. Basically, what this is is if you imagine a really long trace, so a really long roll out of an agent thinking through the problem and making tool calls and fetching context and doing all sorts of things and then at the end of that, the agent gets the right answer. The way reinforcement learning works is that it's going to mark that entire roll out that entire trace as good even if there are a lot of mistakes or a lot of bad things that happened in that trace. So if there were like failed tool calls or hallucinations or even just downright false reasoning as long as the agent ends up with the right answer that leads to the entire trace being marked as good. This introduces a lot of problems and complexity as these rollouts get longer and longer. Also, there's a controversy whether reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards actually yields new reasoning capabilities or if it's just amplifying existing patterns from pre-training. People are still going back and forth on this. And then finally, it's unclear whether the response length increase is actually from the model getting smarter and smarter or if it's just an artifact of this reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards objective that we've created. And so despite their limitations, reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards is still a very exciting area of industry and research. So, in the next video of this series, I'm going to walk through an example of fine-tuning an LLM using RLVR. And to do this, I'm going to use OpenAI's reinforcement fine-tuning API.
